chunks.text: August 10, 2022: CS Prof. Vern Paxson has won the USENIX Security Test of Time Award. Originally published in 1998, Prof. Paxson’s paper, “Bro: A System for Detecting Network Intruders in Real-Time,” was selected for its lasting impact on the research community and by traditional publication metrics; as of this writing, “Bro” has…
chunks.question: What award did Vern Paxson win for his 1998 paper?
chunks.answer: USENIX Security Test of Time Award

chunks.text: July 21, 2022: CS graduate student Xin Lyu (advisors: Jelani Nelson and Avishay Tal) has won the Best Student Paper Award at the Computational Complexity Conference (CCC) 2022. The solo-authored paper titled “Improve Pseudorandom Generators for AC^0 Circuits” was one of two co-winners of the Best Student Paper Award at CCC, which is…
chunks.question: What award did Xin Lyu win at CCC 2022?

chunks.text: June 3, 2022: Two of EECS Prof. Ren Ng’s former graduate students, Pratul Srinivasan and Benjamin Mildenhall, jointly received an honorable mention for the 2021 Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) Doctoral Dissertation Award. This award is presented annually to the “author(s) of the best doctoral dissertation(s) in computer science and engineering.” Srinivasan and…
chunks.question: What recognition did Srinivasan and Mildenhall receive?
chunks.answer: ACM Doctoral Dissertation Award honorable mention

chunks.text: March 24, 2022: A paper with lead author EECS postdoc Efrat Shimron and co-authors EECS graduate student Ke Wang, UT Austin professor Jonathan Tamir (EECS PhD ’18), and EECS Prof. Michael Lustig shows that algorithms trained using “off-label” or misapplied massive, open-source datasets are subject to integrity-compromising biases. The study, which was published in…
chunks.question: What issue does the paper by Efrat Shimron address?
chunks.answer: Bias in AI from off-label dataset use

chunks.text: March 23, 2022: A new type of high-resolution LiDAR chip developed by EECS Prof. Ming Wu could lead to a new generation of powerful, low-cost 3D sensors for autonomous cars, drones, robots, and smartphones. The paper, which appeared in the journal Nature, was co-authored by his former graduate students Xiaosheng Zhang (Ph.D. ’21)…
chunks.question: What technology did Ming Wu develop?

chunks.text: December 20, 2021: CS Profs. Alistair Sinclair and Shafi Goldwasser have won inaugural Test of Time awards at the 2021 Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC), sponsored by the ACM Special Interest Group on Algorithms and Computation Theory (SIGACT). Sinclair won the 20 Year award for his paper, “A polynomial-time approximation algorithm for the…
chunks.question: What award did Sinclair and Goldwasser win?

chunks.text: December 20, 2021: CS alumna and Prof. Shafi Goldwasser (Ph.D. ’84, advisor: Manuel Blum) has won the 2021 Foundations of Computer Science (FOCS) Test of Time Award. This award “recognizes papers published in past Annual IEEE Symposia on Foundations of Computer Science (FOCS) for their substantial, lasting, broad, and currently relevant impact. Papers…
chunks.question: Which award did Shafi Goldwasser win in 2021?

chunks.text: December 7, 2021: EECS Prof. Murat Arcak and his graduate student He Yin have won the second Systems & Control Letters (SCL) Brockett-Willems Outstanding Paper Award. Their paper, “Reachability analysis using dissipation inequalities for uncertain nonlinear systems,” published in SCL Volume 142, on August 2020, was deemed the best of 295 papers submitted…
chunks.question: What award did Arcak and Yin win?

chunks.text: December 2, 2021: EECS Prof. and dean of Engineering Tsu-Jae King Liu has written an opinion piece for the Mercury News in which she explains why “the country urgently needs to reinvest in semiconductor design and manufacturing, including the development of a highly trained workforce.” She argues that America’s lack of a skilled…
chunks.question: What does Tsu-Jae King Liu advocate for?

chunks.text: November 30, 2021: EECS Prof. Emeritus Lotfi Zadeh (1921 – 2017) is being honored with a Google Doodle feature today. In 1964, Zadeh conceived a new mathematical concept called fuzzy logic which offered an alternative to rigid yes-no logic in an effort to mimic how people see the world. He proposed using imprecise…
chunks.question: What concept did Lotfi Zadeh develop?
